# Questions in Livestream 153

Live Stream #153: Philosophy Is A Walk On A Slippery Rock

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=839" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:59</a> | What do you think of the argument from embarrassment? | He thinks it has real value, though not as a perfect stand-alone proof, because embarrassing details often mark less mythologized reporting. | He describes the argument as the claim that unflattering details are less likely in sanitized, lionizing accounts. He thinks the criterion has merit because embarrassing origin traditions tend to be more mundane and less mythologized, while still allowing that agendas can sometimes amplify embarrassment against leaders. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:44</a> | Are `agape`, `phileo`, and `eros` clearly distinct in first-century Greek? | He says the distinctions had largely flattened by the first century, so the words can overlap heavily in practice. | He says older distinctions existed, but by the apostolic and early church period the words had moved close enough in meaning that `phileo` and `agape` could mean essentially the same thing. He also notes that even `eros`, despite later erotic associations, could appear in non-erotic senses such as desire for righteousness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1247" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:47</a> | Was Jesus' conversation with Peter about feeding the flock originally in Greek or Aramaic? | He says the transcript cannot establish which language was original; either Greek or Aramaic is possible in that setting. | He says both Jesus and Peter could have functioned in a polyglot Palestinian setting where Greek, Hebrew, Aramaic, Latin, and other languages circulated. Because Greek was widely used, including in temple contexts, he treats either Greek or Aramaic as possible and says the original language cannot be established from the question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1432" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:52</a> | In Leviticus 19:10, what nuance does the word for "sojourner" carry? | He says it refers to a protected non-Israelite sojourner, not merely a random stranger. | He explains the form as `ger` with prefixed conjunction and preposition, and defines a `ger` as someone permitted to be in the land and to make a living from it. In Leviticus 19, looking forward to Israel occupying the land, he says it means a non-Israelite sojourner or protected alien rather than an unknown stranger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1661" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:41</a> | Is a sojourner more like a traveler or a wanderer? | He says a sojourner is more like a permitted wanderer living off the land than a passing traveler. | He distinguishes a sojourner from a traveler by saying the sojourner wanders through the land with permission, raising sheep or cattle and making a living from the land. That status carries rights and protection, unlike hostile outsiders who entered to raid or pillage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:13</a> | Could women foreigners take part in Passover? | Yes; he says women could participate, with a much lighter ritual requirement than circumcision. | He answers yes and says foreign women could participate even if their husbands did not. He contrasts the male requirement of circumcision with the much lighter female requirement, which he identifies with washing through a ritual bath. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:41</a> | When God "remembers" in passages like Genesis 9:15, is that anthropomorphic? | He says it is better understood as God deciding to act, not as God passively recalling forgotten information. | He says divine remembering is active rather than passive: God already knows, but now chooses to put the matter at the forefront of his agenda and act. He illustrates it with God remembering his promise to Abraham and hearing his people's cries, meaning that the time for action has arrived. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=2072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:32</a> | Did rich Egyptians live beside poor Egyptians, and did priests live in temples? | Priests did not live in temples, and housing could be socially stratified, with better and worse districts like later cities. | He says priests lived in houses, often nearby in the case of high priests, while other priestly personnel rotated in from homes elsewhere. For housing, he says Egyptian towns had better and worse districts, so wealthy and poor residents were not necessarily side by side, and class resentment would have been suppressed under a brutal state. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=2505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:45</a> | How should Genesis 6:6's language of God regretting or grieving be interpreted? | He frames it positionally: God reacts to iniquity with anger and grief while remaining unchanged in essence. | He interprets the regret and grief language as positional, tied to God's proximity to iniquity rather than a change in divine essence. In Genesis 6, he says God remains present in sacred space while human violence and rebellion press in, so God must either withdraw further or judge, and the flood narrative shows him choosing judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=3109" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:49</a> | How should the gift of prophecy be understood today? | He says prophecy is mostly about rightly interpreting the present, not mainly predicting the future. | He argues that most biblical prophecy is not future prediction but clarification of confusing present circumstances. Applied today, he describes prophecy as a kind of discernment about current events, helping people understand what is happening now in light of God's truth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=3676" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:16</a> | Do 1 Corinthians 2:3-5 and 4:20 mean teachers cannot add anything of themselves? | He says Paul warns against faith resting on clever rhetoric rather than God's power, not against all teaching or explanation. | He reads Paul as rejecting persuasive cleverness that makes faith depend on rhetoric rather than on Christ crucified and the power of the gospel. He does not treat this as a ban on evidence, examples, or explanation, but as a warning that teaching should be marked by fidelity to the gospel rather than prideful puffery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=4094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:14</a> | What was the bigger contribution, Assassin's Creed Origins or the spam jerky? | He says Assassin's Creed Origins wins because it funds weeks of programming and equipment upgrades for future work. | He jokes that the spam jerky was appreciated, but says the Assassin's Creed Origins support provides eight weeks of programming and helps fund a new full-frame camera. He connects that equipment upgrade to future museum photography, books, and ministry work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=4334" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:14</a> | Was the torn temple curtain about access to God's throne room, the Spirit going out, or both? | He says both: the sacred is opened to the world and believers gain access to the throne room. | He says the tearing of the veil both releases the sacred into the profane world and gives believers access to the throne room. Because Christ's blood sanctifies, the Holy Spirit can dwell with and within believers without immediate judgment, and believers become representatives of sacred space in the world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=4908" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:48</a> | Why is the Nile so dirty? | He says seasonal rains and runoff carry material down the Nile, a condition he treats as long-standing rather than modern only. | He attributes the Nile's condition to runoff from seasonal rains in eastern Africa carrying contaminants downriver, and says this has long been true rather than only a modern problem. He emphasizes practical danger from parasites and disease, warning travelers to avoid drinking, swimming in, or otherwise ingesting Nile water. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=5473" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:13</a> | What do you think of the conflict thesis and Dawkins's recent "cultural Christian" comments? | He treats Dawkins's comments as inconsistent and says stripping away Christianity leaves a vacuum that should not surprise anyone when something else fills it. | He rejects the conflict thesis as a category error, comparing science's explanation of how coffee boils with the personal reason that someone wanted coffee. He says science and Christianity address different aspects of reality, while Dawkins wants Christian cultural fruit after attacking the tree that produced it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=6516" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:36</a> | Why is the red heifer important? | He says the red heifer is required for temple-purification water and thus for inaugurating a functioning temple. | He says the red heifer's ashes are mixed into holy water used for purification, especially when inaugurating temple tools and space. Because a functioning Jewish temple requires that purification process, he treats the red heifer as a necessary early sacrifice for temple restoration, with strict qualifications such as no blemish and no white hairs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=6687" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:27</a> | What does it mean to be human in Christianity? | He gives a brief Christian-anthropology answer: humans bear God's image and are meant for relationship with God. | He frames humanity as both physical and spiritual, with access to both worlds as embodied spirits. In Christian identity, humans are made to know and serve God, bring glory to him, and represent him as his image bearers wherever they go. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=6839" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:59</a> | What are the problems with skepticism overall? | He says skepticism becomes self-defeating when it is not skeptical of its own skepticism and narrows evidence too far. | He says skepticism can be healthy, but its major failure is refusing to scrutinize itself. When skepticism restricts acceptable evidence so severely that nothing can persuade, he says it collapses into a solipsistic frame where personal feeling becomes the only truth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=6976" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:16</a> | What is your favorite argument for God's existence? | He says he does not have a favorite classical argument and instead leans evidentialist, following the preponderance of evidence. | He says he is not mainly a philosopher of religion and does not find the classical arguments especially useful for his own faith. Instead, he treats belief in God as an inductive, evidential conclusion based on the preponderance of evidence, while acknowledging that arguments such as Plantinga's ontological argument can still be interesting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=7592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:32</a> | What do you think of reading Genesis 2 as a more detailed account of Genesis 6? | He says he no longer favors that reading and expects pushback when people first encounter toledot and consecutive-vav arguments. | He says people often resist unfamiliar tools such as toledot, consecutive-vav formulas, and clan identity because they have not heard them before. He no longer reads Genesis 2 as a continuation of day six, because the formula points to continuity after the prior sequence, possibly after day seven, avoiding a two-creation-accounts reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=8147" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:47</a> | How should readers approach the church fathers? | He says slowly and attentively, because their long-form arguments assume a level of retention and patience that modern readers often lack. | He warns that the church fathers often quote opponents at length before answering them, so readers who jump into the middle can mistake a quoted heretic for the father's own view. He recommends starting with shorter early writings, such as the opening volumes of Schaff's Ante-Nicene Fathers, and building the reading skills needed for longer, more complex works. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=8549" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:29</a> | Did ancient Egyptian have regional variation? | He says probably yes, but the lack of vowels and limited Lower Egyptian material make the details hard to reconstruct. | He says regional variation is likely because Egypt was large and different regions had different contacts, especially the Delta with Semitic and Libyan influences. The difficulty is that Egyptian writing lacks vowels and Lower Egyptian evidence is sparse, so the exact variation cannot be reconstructed confidently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=9029" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:29</a> | What do you think of the Orthodox Jewish claim that both written and oral Torah were given at Sinai? | He accepts written Torah at Sinai but rejects the claim that the later oral Torah was given there. | He allows that two written copies were associated with Sinai, one with the ark and another for copying, but rejects identifying that with an oral Torah. He describes oral Torah as a later developing tradition of teachings, much of it Hellenistic or later, eventually incorporated into the Talmud and changing up to about 500 AD. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=9165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:45</a> | How should readers compare fatherhood in 1 John 3:1 and brotherhood in Hebrews 2:1? | He points to adoption as God's children and co-heirship with Christ, while noting the question is too large for a full treatment. | He connects 1 John 3:1 to adoption: believers are loved by the Father, called children of God, and made co-heirs with Christ. For Hebrews 2, he says Christ is like humans in his humanity and is not ashamed to call the sanctified his brothers, but he warns against turning that solidarity into the claim that humans are divine in the same way Christ is. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=9542" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:02</a> | Were Egyptians better at portraying animals than medieval European artists? | He says it depends on style, purpose, artist, and medium; material limitations matter more than a simple better-or-worse comparison. | He says the comparison depends on artistic purpose, the individual artist, and available media. Medieval artists could be on par with Egyptians before oil painting, but Renaissance oil techniques allowed different effects; many limits in both ancient and medieval art come from materials such as tempera, gouache, and relief media. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=10531" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:31</a> | What do you think about universalism, annihilationism, and eternal conscious torment, and which apologists do you recommend? | He says he holds passive eternal conscious torment, is unconvinced by annihilationism, and recommends several apologetics channels. | He recommends Inspiring Philosophy, Sentinel Apologetics, BK Apologist, and related channels, then distinguishes universalism, annihilationism, and eternal conscious torment. He holds passive eternal conscious torment, meaning separation from God and the pain of holiness to the reprobate, while saying annihilationism and universalism do not fit the evidence as he sees it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=12127" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:07</a> | Is there a majestic plural case ending in Egyptian grammar? | He says not really; majestic plural is more characteristic of Semitic languages, though he leaves room for missed examples. | He says majestic or intensive plurals are not something he has encountered in Egyptian, and he associates them more with Semitic languages than Egyptian, which he describes as Afro-Asiatic rather than purely Semitic. He leaves open the possibility that an example exists somewhere, but says he does not know one offhand. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=12241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:01</a> | Are any Egyptian cognates plausible equivalents to the Hebrew Yahweh? | He says no clear Egyptian equivalent works, because Egyptian-Hebrew transliteration rules do not support the common proposals. | He rejects the common link between Yahweh and the Egyptian moon god Iah because the expected Hebrew-Egyptian transliteration rules do not match: yod, aleph, and the final h sounds would not line up as claimed. He says the Shasu of Yahu inscription is closer, though still not problem-free, while the moon-god proposal is not close phonemically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=12730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:10</a> | Why would Pharaoh expect Hebrew midwives to kill Israelite babies? | He says royal commands in Egypt were backed by force, so the expectation itself fits the culture even if the women bravely refused. | He says Pharaoh expected obedience because he was king in a military dictatorship, and Egyptian authorities commonly used beatings before interrogation or questioning. He treats Shiphrah and Puah's refusal as courageous precisely because they likely risked severe punishment and personal suffering by not complying. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=12595" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:55</a> | Were the midwives in Exodus 1 Egyptian or Israelite? | He says they were Israelite, because Shiphrah and Puah are Semitic rather than Egyptian names. | He says Shiphrah and Puah are Hebrew or Semitic names, not Egyptian names or Israelite transliterations from Egyptian. Shiphrah appears in a Middle Kingdom Semitic slave list, and Puah does not have Egyptian name structure, so he concludes the midwives were Israelites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=13158" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:18</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have anything equivalent to modern slang? | He says yes, late Egyptian functions as a street-language development from more formal Middle Egyptian. | He describes Middle Egyptian as high, formal language and late Egyptian as a street-level development that emerged around the Second Intermediate Period. He notes major syntactic differences, including subject-verb-object patterns, and says late Egyptian eventually became the standard language of New Kingdom Egyptian culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=13770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:30</a> | Did Egyptian husbands and wives sleep in the same bed? | Yes, if the bed was large enough; he says double beds and shared sleeping arrangements are plausible in the evidence. | He says husbands and wives did sleep together and that fragments of double beds are known. Since Egyptian sleeping arrangements used headrests rather than pillows, a shared bed was plausible when the bed was large enough to accommodate both people and their headrests. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=13955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:35</a> | Are the Twelve Prophets one book or twelve separate books? | He says they are twelve separate books, though often bound together because they are short. | He answers that the Twelve Prophets are originally twelve separate books. They are often bound together as one collection because the individual books are short, but that collection format does not make them one original book. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=13988" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:08</a> | What examples of majestic or intensive plural appear in Semitic languages outside the Bible? | He gives a Talmudic example using a plural form for "great king." | He gives a Talmudic example referring to David as `melakim`, taking the plural form of king as a majestic plural meaning great king. He presents it as an example of this plural usage outside the biblical text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=14132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:32</a> | Did ancient people understand that languages could be related? | He says they seem to have had some practical sense of language relationships and dialect closeness, even without modern comparative linguistics. | He says ancient people seem to have recognized some language relationships, such as Aramaic and Syriac, Amorite and Hebrew, and dialects of Akkadian. He qualifies that they did not always understand how languages were related or how close some relationships were, but they had at least some practical concept of related languages. |
